Next time I will make the hiring manager do a personality test just to get an interview with me. I swear to God."
""
"Applying for simple jobs should be just that: simple. I just spent 30 minutes answering 200 questions to apply for a warehouse job. I have a forklift license. I can lift heavy things. I show up on time. That should be enough. Don't trust me? Let me show you."
""
"Before anyone read my CV, before anyone looked at my cover letter, I was asked to prove my personality was the right kind. Spontaneous or structured? Risk-taker or rule-follower? Predictable or creative? I answered honestly, knowing that was probably a stupid choice. But I want to feel that I can be myself, that there is no wrong personality."
""
"The verdict came back. And I saw directly that they were never going to hire me. My ADHD made me sound like a teenager still living in his parents' basement, not able to follow any rules, just bouncing from one thing to another. And yes, once I was that person in my teens, when I didn't know what neurodiversity was. ADHD made me behave in unpredictable ways. It is still part of my personality — but it doesn't mean I can't handle a regular job, that I don't know how to follow rules, that I don't show up on time, or that I'm untrustworthy."
""
"All of that stuff that is natural for others, I had to learn the hard way. Learn the consequences. Learn how to operate under stress. How to be organized, count time, deliver what you promised. I can do all of those things now, no problem. But ask me what I prefer: strict rules or flexibility? No question. I know which one I prefer. I also know which one is the right answer. So there I am, staring at the screen, knowing the "correct" click, wondering if I'm applying for a job or auditioning for a personality I don't have."
""
"The system was never designed to include me. It was designed for normies, and me not being one means I need to pretend. But here's the thing that really gets me: every job posting says the same thing. We embrace diversity. Everyone is welcome. All personalities valued. Then they run every single applicant through the same personality keyhole before a human has even glanced at the CV. That's not embracing diversity. That's performing it."
""
"So what's the solution?"
"We keep pretending, or we let AI fill in the answers for us. Or — and this is what I'm actually asking for — employers stop using these tests, at least not as step zero for every applicant. There is plenty of scientific evidence they don't even work. Save them for the final round if you must. But stop making someone with a forklift license prove their soul before anyone's read their name.

What if going to a job interview felt more like going on a date?"
"Both sides nervous but excited. Both sides curious about the other person, not just the CV. Both sides with skin in the game, genuinely trying to figure out if this could work. Right now the hiring interview feels nothing like that."
""
"I talked to Olga Engvall, who is building MinglMe, a new kind of dating app. "

Who you gonna call? "
"(For honest feedback)"
""
"Colleagues are too polite, clients too careful."
""
"The ones who give me brutally honest feedback are my wife and kids. But when it comes to how good I am at interviewing people, they're just not in the room."
"I've done over 20 research interviews now. At some point I stopped and asked: am I actually any good at this? I already knew one thing about myself. I talk too much. Fine for filling a silence, terrible when your whole job is to shut up and listen."
""
"But what else? What am I doing well? What should I keep and what should I change? Without honest feedback I'm just guessing."
""
"So I asked my second omniscience. Claude has sat in on every interview. It knows the questions, the context, the patterns across all 20+ conversations. I asked it straight: what am I doing well and what do I need to work on?"
""
"What you get depends entirely on what you ask for. Ask for a summary and you get a polite summary. Ask for the uncomfortable version and you get that instead. The AI is not going to volunteer the hard stuff. You have to actually want it. And I wanted it."
""
"The part that stuck: "You're not asking the second question. When someone says something sharp, you move on. That's where the insight actually lives.""
"I read that three times. It was right. The interviewee can't see it. I can't see it mid-session. You need someone who's been in every conversation and isn't in any of them."
""
"A machine has no stake in your reaction. No relationship to protect. It just tells you what it sees. Don't treat it like a cheerleader. It can be your toughest critic and your most honest colleague. You just have to ask the right question."
""
"I ain't afraid of no feedback.

All those promises on LinkedIn: "
""Build this in 2 minutes." "
""What used to take days now takes minutes.""
""
"On the surface, great. On closer look, two problems."
""
"Problem A: It doesn't actually work as shown. "
"The setup is more complicated. The result is more simplistic. It doesn't fit your process, your team's tools, the careful workflows you've spent years refining. The AI tool that promised to save you hours is unpredictable in ways the demo never showed."
""
"Problem B: It works exactly as promised."
"Wait. Wasn't that the goal?"
"Yes. But the moment something is genuinely that easy, everyone can do it. In 2 minutes. With no prior knowledge. So what exactly are you selling? When the app stores are flooded with the same idea built by a thousand different people who all watched the same demo, the effort is zero and so is the value."
""
"I've been building my own product with these tools. The parts that used to take me the longest, the layout, the boilerplate, the repetitive logic, the AI handles those now. "
""
"Fine. But I still spend most of my time on the judgment calls. Knowing what to ask for. Recognizing when the output is plausible but wrong. Deciding what matters."
""
"That part doesn't compress. It might actually be getting harder, because now there's more output to evaluate and fewer obvious errors to catch."
"Time compression is real. You will get faster. That part of the promise holds. But speed without judgment is just faster noise."
""
"The tricky part is not finding the right AI tool. It's knowing when efficiency tips over into deficiency.

Agents can help you build the wrong thing really, really fast."
""
"Everywhere you look, people are bragging about how many agents they have running while they sleep. Building everything and anything. If you're not burning tokens, you're falling behind."
""
"I resisted the urge. Went old school instead. Talking to humans about human needs. No prototype, no agents running overnight. Just 40 conversations."
""
"Turned out I was quite a bit off. My original idea was a solution to a problem, just not a good one. It lacked real differentiation and a strong reason to switch."
""
"What replaced it is cleaner, more focused, and honestly easier to build. I now know exactly what I'm solving, for whom, and why. The research didn't just inform the product. It became the product."
""
"Talking to real people used to be table stakes. Now it's buried somewhere between AI-generated personas, synthetic research, and the pressure to ship something, anything."
""
"Agents can build fast. But fast in the wrong direction is still wrong."
"Suddenly talk is not cheap anymore. Building is.

Amy Webb just killed the trend report. "
"Long live the Convergence Outlook. "
""
"Watching her annual SXSW presentation used to be my highlight of the year. No better way to get a glimpse of the future — the hopeful and the dystopian. So hearing her say it was over hit me hard."
"What she replaced it with is better. Instead of tracking individual trends, she now tracks convergences — multiple forces colliding into something irreversible. Less trend report, more storm tracker."
""
"Three insights that moved my perspective: "
"1. Your body is becoming a product surface. "
"Exoskeletons, AR glasses, gene editing. Some people will be objectively more capable than others. Opting out — for money, values, or health — will increasingly mean falling behind. We've been designing for a neutral user. That user is disappearing."
""
"2. Unlimited labor breaks the job story. "
"Agentic AI and lights-out factories produce output without people. GDP up, unemployment up — at the same time. The next internet is being built for agents to transact with each other, with humans designed out. Interaction design becomes interaction governance."
""
"3. Loneliness is a market. "
"Dependency is the product. LLMs are already the largest mental health provider in the US. Platforms don't just want your attention — they want to own how you feel before you think, vote, or buy. Emotional regulation isn't a UX side effect. It's a design surface. And almost nobody is designing it responsibly."
""
"Watch the full presentation. Download the Convergence Outlook free at ftsg.com. "
"Both are long. "
"Both are worth it."

Do you remember this meme?"
"The sender: I just wrote a whole email from a bullet point. "
"The receiver: I just summarized that email into one bullet point."
""
"We laughed and moved on. But I keep thinking about it. I believe it's not a meme, it's a prophecy. "
""
"I use AI to help me write. I research faster, structure better, get unstuck when I'm staring at a blank page. The output is more polished than it would be without it. My AI-cowriter points out inconsistencies, redundancies or when I'm too passive aggressive (yep, that happens). "
"But I know that somewhere out there, someone is probably using AI to summarize this post into three bullet points."
""
"Which means there's a real chance that what I spent an hour thinking about gets dissolved into a sentence. Fed into the next person's context. Forgotten by the algorithm before anyone actually read it."
""
"I started writing on LinkedIn to think out loud, to inspire and find people thinking about similar things. That still happens — someone writes a personal comment or a DM that makes me reconsider something. Those moments feel real and makes it worth while. "
""
"But mostly I'm stuck with this question: "
"Am I writing to a person, or am I just feeding the algorithm?

It's been 10 months since I left my last design job, and I still think of myself as a designer. Just not the kind UX job ads are looking for anymore."
"I opened three senior UX roles this week. All of them asked for AI skills like proficiency in Figma Make, using ChatGPT to go faster, and confidence in AI-assisted wireframing."
""
"That's not wrong. It's just describing 2019 design work with a 2026 plugin installed."
""
"Here's what's actually changed for me and how I use AI:"
"👉 As a tool: I tell it to do specific things so I can focus on the parts that need me. "
"👉 As a co-worker: I ask for opinions, pushback, a second read on decisions I'm not sure about. "
"👉 As a mentor: to teach me things I don't know, fill gaps I'd normally paper over, challenge assumptions I've held for years."
""
"That last one mattered most when I started teaching and hit a wall I didn't see coming. My instinct as a product designer is always to remove friction: smoother flows, less resistance, faster to the outcome."
"But learning doesn't work that way, the friction IS the point. The right amount of resistance triggers thought, builds memory, and makes knowledge stick. I needed someone to challenge that old reflex. So I built a dedicated thinking partner: a combined pedagogy and UX expert I could work through the course with. Lesson by lesson, asking why the order mattered, where I was making it too easy, too hard, or too complex. Learning how to stitch separate lessons into something that holds together for both the learner and the school."
""
"What job ads don't ask, and probably should:"
"How do you produce more value with AI, not just more output? "
"How does AI make you a better designer, not just a faster one? "
"And how do you delegate without giving away your thinking, your principles, or the human touch?"
""
"Those are the questions I'm figuring out in real time. Harder than learning a new tool. Far more interesting.

"I made a prototype and it failed.""
""Nice! Congratulations! What did you learn?""
""
"That's a completely normal conversation in UX. Failure is the point. You build to discover what work and what doesn't. BOTH are equally important. Your test, learn, iterate. "
""
"Now replace "prototype" with "AI pilot" and watch the room panic."
"95% of AI pilot projects fail, says ONE study. Apparently this is a crisis. The AI sky is falling. 😱 "
""
"Here is the thing. "
"The actual problem isn't the failure rate. It's the expectation. Companies treat AI like a magic pill — slap it onto existing workflows, skip the messy middle, wait for money to rain from the sky. Then act surprised when a test behaves like a test."
""
"A pilot is a prototype. It's supposed to break. That's what it's for."
"Stop mourning the 95%. Get obsessed with the 5% that worked. Find out exactly why. Then build from there."
""
"That's how UX has always worked. AI is no different. We already had Lean Startup and Lean UX. Now it's time for Lean AI.

Should designers, I mean developers learn how to code?"
"Wait what? Of course developers should learn to code. what kind of stupid question is that?"
"- Yesterday the answer would be YES, without a doubt."
"- Today, it’s still a Yes with a caveat."
"- Tomorrow it will be a Maybe."
""
"Take a car for example. You need a driving license to drive it but don’t need to become a mechanic. Understanding how your car works means you will be a better driver and a better car owner. You will make sure your car is in great condition, know what things you can fix by yourself and what needs an expert mechanic."
""
"Leaning to code will still be valuable, but not necessary. Writing code by hand is quickly becoming an exception. Be able to read and understand code, the structures and how it works. That are the skills that are now more valuable than pushing out more lines of code. This is not a hypothetical future. Look at Anthropic or Spotify, with almost 0 manual coding. Todays outliers are the norms of tomorrow. "
""
"The question isn't "Should designers or developers learn to code?" "
"The question is: "How much do I need to know to work effectively with AI?" "
"And the answer is: Enough to be dangerous. Enough to diagnose. Enough to drive.

Why the "UX Designer" role is disappearing"
""
"The LinkedIn is filled with UX people panicking about being redundant or accusing companies of not caring about users. "
""
"I have a different perspective: ”UX Designer" was never the destination. It was a solution to a problem we no longer have."
""
"Think about how the UX-role emerged. Products got complex. Teams grew. No single person could own the whole thing anymore, so we divided it up. UX here. UI there. Research in a separate room. Front-end over the wall. Everyone did their piece and handed it to the next person, hoping the original intent survived the translation. The specialization made sense. It was a necessary response to scale and constraint."
""
"But somewhere along the way we forgot it was a workaround. We turned it into an identity. A reason to stop at the wireframe and call it done. "Now we design the user experience. Hooray! ""
""
"The designers thriving right now are treating UX as strategic problem solving, not deliverable production, not just building legos with design systems. What AI can't do (at least not yet) is judge. It can't taste. It can't understand context the way someone who has met real users. "
""
"The (AI) tools now let one person be strategic, do the research, sketch the flows, build a coded prototype almost indistinguishable from the final product, and ship it. The constraint that created the UX silo is dissolving. So the label is dissolving with it."
""
"That's what Shopify and Duolingo understood. They didn't remove UX thinking — it's baked into everything they do. They removed the fence that said UX thinking stops here and someone else takes over."
""
"The workaround is over. The real work just got bigger.

There's a post going viral right now, at least in the AI community. It’s written by a developer Matt Shumer, called "Something Big Is Happening.""
""
"It’s describing the rapidly growing capabilities of agentic AI. Things we all heard before. What's new and surprising is his comparison between AI and COVID—how something can be bubbling underneath the surface while most people don't see it coming. All the signs are there. We just don't want to believe them."
""
"The AI advancements aren't slowing down. They're accelerating. Things that were impossible yesterday are expected today. And the gap between what's possible and what people are prepared for? That gap is widening."
""
"Here's what worries me:"
"My students aren't ready for what's coming. Not because they're not smart or hardworking. But because the curriculum moves slowly, and AI is moving fast."
"What used to be a strength—stable, time-tested education—is now a risk. The promise schools make is: "We'll give you the tools, skills, and mindset to start your career." But if those tools are already outdated by graduation, that promise breaks."
""
"I'm not blaming the schools. Curriculum design takes time. Approval processes take time. Institutional change takes time. That used to work. Now the technology is changing faster than the process can adapt. That's the disconnect."
""
"What developers are experiencing right now:"
"Shumer describes telling AI what he wants built, walking away for hours, and returning to find it done. Better than he would have done it himself. This isn't future speculation—this is his Monday morning. Developers watched AI go from "helpful assistant" to "does my job" in about eighteen months."
""
"Designers, UX people, analysts, writers—they haven't experienced that radical shift yet. But they will. The question isn't if. It's when."
""
"Why I'm building what I'm building:"
"I can't change institutional timelines. But I can teach what students actually need right now. That's why I'm building my AI course for designers. Not to replace what schools do, but to fill the gap between what's taught and what's required. To give students—and working professionals—a way to adapt before the shift hits their field. "
""
"Because here's the truth: the people who figure this out in time will have options. The ones who wait will be scrambling.

How to Learn AI with AI"
"I was just testing to make stuff with AI. Building small apps that broke when things got hard. When I hit a wall, I stopped and moved on. This is a good and cheap way to get started, but will only get you that far. "
"What I needed was structure. A real curriculum that would push me past the walls instead of letting me quit."
""
"Here's what I did:"
"I set up three AI advisors in Claude, each with different skills and objectives:"
"- Product development expert (what to build and why)"
"- Technical mentor (how Lovable actually works)"
"- Learning designer (how to structure lessons that stick)"
"I showed them the scattered projects I'd already built, explained my goals, and asked them to design a course."
""
"They argued with each other. They argued with me, I pushed back. What to start with, in what order, level of detail and mostly importantly, how to make it valuable and engaging. To and fro until we setled on a plan that I liked and believed in. "
""
"The result: a 10-lesson curriculum that started exactly where I was and pushed me exactly where I needed to go. Better than the $2,000 courses I'd taken before — because it was built for me, not for everyone."
""
"So does this mean schools and courses are meaningless?"
"No. Good courses give you the foundation — the basic knowledge and tools you can't skip. But they can't keep up with the pace of change or customize to where you actually are."
""
"That's where AI fills the gap. It doesn't replace structured education. It extends it. You get a custom path forward once you've got the basics."
"It still takes time and effort. But now you're in charge of your own learning journey.

All those wireframes, prototypes, user flows. They were shadows on a wall. Beautiful postcards from an imagined, bright future. But someone else decided if they'd become real. Someone else built them. Or didn't."
""
"I was designing in a simulation."
"Then something shifted."
""
"I built my first actual product last month. Not a prototype. Not a handoff. A real thing that real people can use. I wrote the prompts, debugged the errors, shipped the features. The gap between "I have an idea" and "here's the thing" just... collapsed."
""
"This is what people meant when they said "designers should learn to code." I thought it was about syntax and Git commits. Turns out it's not about that at all. It's about closing the loop. Seeing your idea break in real time, fixing it, watching it work. That immediate feedback cycle."
""
"The Matrix metaphor isn't just for the image. I literally feel like I took a red pill. The old world where I hand off designs and hope they survive, that world still exists. I can see it. But I'm not in it anymore."
""
"I'm not saying everyone needs to build. But if you've ever felt that frustration, you know what I mean. The thing you designed getting lost in translation, simplified, postponed, killed. This is the unlock. AI didn't just give me a tool. It gave me the ability to close my own loops. To go from idea to real thing without waiting for someone else to make it happen."
""
"I'm documenting the whole thing. Ten weeks, ten builds, every wall I hit and how I got past it. Successes and failures, everything that actually happens when you try to build for real. First one drops Friday."
""
"What about you? Still designing in the simulation, or have you started building for real?

Teaching UX Turned Me Into a Learning Designer (And I Had No Idea What I Was Doing)"
""
"This fall I taught two courses for Nackademin — UX/UI and Information Architecture. With my long experience in UX/UI and product design it should have been a piece of cake. Right?"
""
"It turned out to be anything but."
""
"Knowing something is completely different than explaining it in a clear, pedagogical and interesting way.To teach UX design I needed to apply learning design. But where to start and what framework to use? Flipped classroom, Bloom's taxonomy or ADDIE?"
""
"I had so many questions, no answers, and no time to start learning learning design from scratch.AI to the rescue."
""
"I brought the UX expertise. Claude helped me structure it and build out the course in Notion. We worked through Bloom's taxonomy, applied flipped classroom mentality, refined learning objectives, designed assignments, caught gaps I'd missed. Back and forth, iteration after iteration."
"It wasn't AI doing my work. It was co-creation. I knew what I wanted to achieve, inspired by all the good teachers I've had. AI helped me build it faster and more thoroughly than I could have alone, finding gaps and making it all fit together."
""
"🤔 Here's what I learned: AI is best when you already know what good looks like."
"I couldn't have designed these courses without my UX expertise. But I also couldn't have built them this fast, this thoroughly, without Claude as a co-creator."
""
"The designers who win with AI aren't the ones striving for efficiency, doing the same things but faster. They're the ones using their expertise to build things they couldn't build before.

Six months of learning Python, statistics, and ML theory. Zero products shipped. Zero real users. Just tutorials and planning. My board—Pieter Levels, John Maeda, Ethan Mollick, Jason Fried, Herminia Ibarra, Roger Martin, Justin Sung, Blair Enns—people I admire and trust. "
""
"Pieter: "You've been learning for 6 months and shipped zero products. That's not learning, that's hiding.""
""
"He was right. I was stuck in planning limbo. Exactly what I tell my students not to do. Here's what changed: I'm not "a UX designer learning AI" anymore. I'm an AI product builder who understands users deeply. Different identity. Different position. "
""
"The reality: I need a LIA internship by October. I need a portfolio to get it. I need to ship actual products to build a portfolio. So I better get started, like yesterday. "
""
"First project: Writer Coach. Ships in 14 days. It's a tool that coaches you to improve your writing instead of rewriting it for you. Asks questions. Points out what's unclear. Suggests improvements. But you do the work. No AI slop. Better thinking. "
""
"Why this matters: Most AI writing tools replace human thinking. This one amplifies it. AI should make humans better, not obsolete. That's the intersection between UX and AI that I care about. "
""
"Stay tuned for more details about the app. "
""
"PS: Do you want more details on how to build your own board of experts? Let me know in the comments."

The AI Quadrant: "
"Why Both the Hype Squad and the Doomers Are Selling You Half-Truths"
""
"There’s a 2x2 grid I keep thinking about. One axis is optimism-to-pessimism. The other is whether people are understating or overstating what AI actually does. The tech-bros and doomers are both cherry-picking from the same messy reality. The actual work is learning to navigate the jagged edges yourself instead of outsourcing your judgment to either camp. "
""
"Top right: The exaggerated optimism. "
"Home of the tech-bros. Cure cancer, solve poverty, AGI saves humanity (and also makes them rich—total coincidence, right?). The good part? It’s inspiring. It makes you imagine a future beyond our current constraints. The bad part? It’s also convenient. It justifies whatever resources and power grabs happen in the name of “national security” and “human survival.”"
""
"The bottom-right: The exaggerated pessimism. "
"Say hi to AI doomers. AI becomes Skynet, replaces your job, turns the planet into paperclips. Again, there’s something real here—the labor exploitation, environmental costs, copyright theft, and regulatory chaos are actually happening. But exaggeration paralyzes people instead of empowering them to solve the problem."
""
"Top left: The oversimplified optimism: "
"“It’s just a tool, like a pen.” Comforting, sure. But a pen can’t write your paper for you. A pen doesn’t hide what it’s doing inside a black box. The charm of this view is it makes AI less scary. The problem? It lets you stop asking questions."
""
"Bottom left: The oversimplified pessimism: "
"“It’s just a stochastic parrot, nonsense, AI slop.” The value here is warranted skepticism. The problem is it misses what AI actually is good at, right now, and ignores the speed of improvement."
""
"So who’s right? Everyone. And no one."
""
"The jagged frontier of AI (term popularized by Ethan Mollick) is that it excels at some tasks and fails spectacularly at others—often in ways nobody predicted. Right now, the only honest move is to stop letting the zealots and skeptics narrate your relationship with it. Learn what it actually does. Build your own grid. Make your own bets. Just make sure to keep exploring, with both the optimistic AND the skeptical view.

The best way to learn something is to teach it to someone else. This is well known fact, still I get surprised how true it actually is. I just started teaching a short course in Information Architecture for UX students. During my long career as UX designer, IA has never been a big subject. The main focus has bees users tests and prototypes, while the overall information structure has usually been already set (in stone). Is AI Actually the One Ring? 💍 "
"(The AI dilemma)"
"I sometimes get swept up by the rapid pace of AI development and the endless new possibilities it offers. Every new demo or model feels like unlocking a superpower I just have to try. Several AI tools are deeply woven into my daily workflow—I honestly can’t imagine a day without them. The product I’m building wouldn’t even exist without AI."
""
"In other areas of life, I try to make mindful choices: I buy organic or local food, I drive a gas-powered car but avoid flying, and I do my best to choose options that have a positive (or at least neutral) impact."
""
"But when it comes to AI, I feel a bit like Boromir from The Fellowship of the Ring. I want to use this incredible power for good, but I’m not sure it’s possible. What if all my well-intended achievements are ultimately outweighed by AI’s negative side effects?"
""
"There’s no going back to a world without AI. The calls to pause development have been largely ignored. AI skills are now essential to land a job, and AI is being integrated into every product—whether it makes sense or not. Opting out is nearly impossible. And unlike in Tolkien’s story, there’s no Frodo to cast all the LLMs into Mount Doom."
""
"So what do I actually want?"
"I want the ability to make good, informed choices. I want to understand the real costs of my actions and my use of AI. I want transparency, and I want options that let us choose a slower, more sustainable path. I want to be able to do more personally, to reclaim some power from the tech giants and investors who currently hold it all."
""
"The path ahead feels more uncertain than ever. The only thing that’s becoming clearer is the risk of an environmental and economic crash long before we reach the promised land of AGI or Artificial Superintelligence."
""
"It's time for me to dive deeper into Ethical AI and learn how the ring can become what we intended it be: great power with great responsibility.

"The wall"

"Young designers are struggling to get into the industry. Many junior roles are now replaced by AI, and there is fierce competition for those few jobs left. Starting your own business seems more and more like a valid option, even without the experience. It’s definitely going to be hard to find the first clients, a lot of free, hard work, and many mistakes along the way. Even if your product or business fails, you will have learned a lot more, gained business and collaboration skills, self-motivation and the ability to solve your own problems. "

The AI Readiness Quadrant: "
"How to create AI alignment in you organisation. "
""
""If you don't start learning AI, you will soon be replaced." That's the warning from CTOs at Salesforce, Duolingo, and Fiverr. Yet employees using AI often feel they're cheating or that their work is devalued."
""
"So what are we supposed to do? Should AI stay or should AI go?"
"I see this as an alignment problem. The similarities between organizations and schools are striking—there's often misalignment between management and employees. AI maturity creates another dimension. High maturity doesn't mean that you are an AI expert, just that you use AI with intent where it creates value. "
"Let's use my favorite visualization tool: the QUADRANT."
""
"The Four Zones of AI Alignment:"
""
"1️⃣ The Vulnerable Zone (Low Org/Low Employee Readiness)"
"Traditional workflows reign and AI skills are nonexistent. Companies view AI as tomorrow's problem, becoming increasingly vulnerable as the efficiency gap widens."
"For Employees: Start small—dedicate 30 minutes daily to learning relevant AI tools."
"For Organizations: Begin with a low-risk AI pilot addressing a specific pain point."
""
"2️⃣ The Risk Zone (High Org/Low Employee Readiness)"
"Organizations invest heavily in AI while employees lack skills to leverage these tools. This isn't just about ROI—it's about human livelihoods."
"For Employees: Take ownership of your AI education immediately."
"For Organizations: Shift investment toward training and create safe spaces for experimentation."
""
"3️⃣ The Shadow AI Zone (Low Org/High Employee Readiness)"
"Employees quietly adopt AI tools while organizational policies lag behind, creating innovation but also security risks."
"For Employees: Document AI successes and approach leadership with formalization proposals."
"For Organizations: Create pathways to legitimize shadow AI use rather than shutting it down."
""
"4️⃣ The Synergy Zone (High Org/High Employee Readiness)"
"The optimal scenario. Organizations and people move in harmony toward AI-enhanced workflows, creating better experiences for employees and customers alike. "
"For Employees: Develop uniquely human skills that complement AI capabilities."
"For Organizations: Create feedback loops between AI systems and human expertise."
""
"What do you think about this model? Does it make sense? And where does your organization fall on the AI Readiness Quadrant? "

The AI standoff between applicants and hiring managers "
""
"IKEA got tired of all the AI-generated applications and urged candidates to stop using AI. Many companies feel the same. Nobody wants to sort among thousands of generic letters.   "

"Is that going to happen? Are people going to stop using AI for job applications? Not a chance. "

"There are several reasons for that:"
"- The current job market is a mess, and people are doing everything they can to get an upper hand. They don’t cheat because they are lazy, but because they are desperate. "
"- The companies are using AI in the hiring process, so why shouldn’t I? "
"- Applying for a position is a hard, monotonous job, with lousy feedback and no pay.  "
"- Other applicants are using it, so I must as well, just to keep up. "
"- The AI is doing a good job with minimal effort, allowing applicants to invest in quantity instead of quality. "
""
"This will only lead to an arms race where both parties will use even more AI to gain the upper hand. This dilemma is not new. Education was the first to be hit by the disruptive force of AI, making the current ways of knowledge assessment unusable. "
""
"What HR should learn from Education:"
""
"- Design your process from the assumption that EVERYONE is going to use AI. Sooner or later they will. "
"- Detection software doesn’t work, so stop using or relying on it. "
"- Transparency. Be transparent with how you are using AI in your hiring process. "
"- Clear expectations and rules. Educate the applicants, when and how it’s OK to use AI. Show good and bad examples. "
"- Look for biases in your AI tools and make sure that the sorting algorithm is not a black box. "
"- One step in your process needs to be IRL, on-site, in person. This should be the most important step, where AI will not be present (from any part). "
"- Create trust. If you play by the rules, you can demand the same from your audience. "
""
"The applicants need to put in serious work as well, learning how to use A1 in a good and productive way. The companies wants to hire you, NOT your chatbot. "

"The gap wasn't in design skills or execution - it was in understanding design's potential as a strategic force. The designer delivered exactly what was asked for: a more modern, attractive website. And technically, there was nothing wrong with that."
""
"Yet I couldn't shake the missed opportunity. With just a bit more strategic thinking, that same website could have become a robust tool for driving business growth, supporting sales, and delivering real user value. The foundation was there - it just needed a deeper purpose."
""
"Walking away from that collaboration taught me something important: the challenge isn't just about advocating for strategic design - it's about bridging fundamentally different worldviews about design's role in business. Some see it as decoration, others as strategy, and these perspectives often talk past each other."
""
"This disconnect isn't new, but it's still holding organizations back. While there's a growing movement of designers and leaders working to elevate design's strategic role, we need more voices, more examples, and more practical approaches to bridge this gap."
""
"I'm still processing how to tackle this challenge beyond the usual LinkedIn posts. How do we help both designers and business leaders see and unlock design's full potential? How do we make strategic design thinking accessible and valuable to those who haven't yet embraced it?"
""
"What's your experience with this disconnect? Have you found effective ways to shift the conversation from aesthetics to strategy? I'd love to hear your stories and insights.

Suddenly, things are not what they used to be. "
"I’m not who I used to be. "
"What happened? And when? "
""
"I’m sitting at my desk, browsing through the available UX jobs. Jobs that describe exactly what I’m good at and have done for many years. I should feel energised and excited, even if the chances of actually getting the job are minuscule. I always liked a challenge. No, it’s something else. The universe, or my gut, is trying to tell me something. "
""
"But if I’m not a UX designer, then who am I?"
""
"I always found it problematic to tie one’s identity to the title. So let’s start with reframing the question. I’m still a designer; I just don’t want to do the traditional UX stuff. At least not have that as the main objective. I want something different. The new question should be: If I’m not a UX designer, what kind of designer am I?"
""
"Just to be clear, I’m not looking for a new title. I believe I had them all. A title doesn’t fill your life with purpose, at least not mine. I’m looking for a path. "
""
"It’s time for a change and to figure out what I actually want. "
"I’ll start with a mini Ikigai and map out things I enjoy doing, things I want to do, and things I know. Let’s keep the money out of the equation for a moment. The next step is to build something useful from things in the middle. "
""
"I think I already have an idea. I’ll keep you posted.

The 5 Levels of Design: From Craft to Impact"
""
"1. The Work You Do: The Output"
"This is ground zero - the actual design work you produce. We're talking wireframes, sketches, prototypes, and components. It's usually what gets us hired (hello portfolio!) and how our day-to-day work gets evaluated. Think of it as your design craftsmanship."
"As designers, we often start here as juniors, mastering our tools and building that foundation. But here's the thing - while this level is crucial, it's just the beginning of what design can be."
""
"2. How You Work: The Process"
"Gone are the days when pretty pictures alone could tell your story. Now, it's all about the thinking behind those pixels. How did you get there? What problems were you solving? Your process reveals your understanding of user needs and shows whether you're working through creative chaos or following a thoughtful, consistent approach."
""
"3. How You Work With Others: The Teamwork Game"
"Let's be real - design is a team sport. Even if you're the lone designer in your company, you need others to bounce ideas off of and validate your work. This level is about being that person everyone wants on their project."
"It goes beyond just design skills - it's about collaboration, stepping up when needed, continuous learning, and yes, even putting away that coffee mug (yours or not!). The real question is: do people light up when they hear you're joining their team?"
""
"4. Your Business Impact"
"Here's where we zoom out and look at the bigger picture. Understanding design's role in your company isn't just nice-to-have - it's essential. Forget seeing business as the "other side." You're there to help the business serve its customers better, period. The better you understand this connection, the more impact your design work can have."
""
"5. Your Impact on Humanity"
"Sounds heavy, right? But hear me out. Every design choice you make, every company you work for, every project you take on - it all adds up. We're not just pushing pixels; we're shaping experiences that affect real people's lives. Making sure that impact is positive? That's on us."
""
"Wrapping It Up"
"There's a pattern here: you start with your craft and gradually expand your view to see the bigger picture. The cool thing is, you don't have to leave one level before moving to the next - you can work on multiple levels simultaneously. The goal? Being able to look back one day and feel proud of your impact across all these dimensions."

Things that took me far too long to realize: You don’t need stuff to do stuff."
""
"I used to believe that to excel as a photographer, I needed the best camera in the market. Hours were spent researching, buying books, and taking classes to perfect the technicalities. However, the camera ended up collecting dust on a shelf. The same pattern emerged in other areas—seeking more creativity, responsibility, or leadership roles. The truth is, you don’t need fancy tools or titles to start."
""
"Have you found yourself in a similar loop? Investing in pricey gym memberships for a beach body or waiting for your boss to notice your potential? The reality is, you can start where you are with what you have. Want to enhance your photography skills? Begin by snapping pictures with your phone and analyzing them. "
""
"Dreaming of leadership? Start by helping others and taking more initiative. No need for titles or formal permissions. Extending your care will naturally expand your influence, something no course or gadget can match."
""
"PS: Remember, ensure your actions align with your company's policies. If not, seek permission or adjust your approach accordingly. Start now with what you have, and watch your progress soar. "
""
"#Realization #StartWithWhatYouHave #PersonalGrowth

Things that took me far too long to realise:"
"It’s not the best designer that gets the job"
""
"I used to see the job application process as a race. The quickest, strongest or most experienced designer will get the job just because he or she was better than the rest. If I didn’t get the job it meant that I just wasn’t good enough. "
""
"It took me a while to realise that I was wrong. The hiring process doesn’t work this way. Even if you are by far the most skilled and experienced applicant, it is till not a guarantee for landing the job. "
""
"The reason for this is that the hiring process is situational. It is not the about finding the best designer, but the most suited person, with the most relevant skills to solve certain problems for this specific company. "
""
"In other words, even if you are an excellent hammer you will lose to an average monkey wrench. "
""
"The takeaway"
"Make sure to understand what specific problem the employer is trying to solve with the hire. Even if all the UX designer application texts sound the same, the needs behind them are different. If you can briefly and concretely explain why you are the best solution for that need, you my friend might, might just landed the job.

More Than Just Pixels: Why Design Needs Strategy"
""
"I used to think that the only thing that mattered as a designer was creating beautiful, tangible artifacts. Meetings, strategy sessions, and research were just roadblocks to the fun stuff. But I've learned that this approach is shortsighted."
""
"Why Design Needs Strategy:"
"⚫ Purpose: Without a clear goal, your design might miss the mark."
"⚫ Efficiency: A plan helps everyone work together, avoiding wasted time and resources."
"⚫ Alignment: Meetings aren't just for sharing information; they build a sense of community."
"⚫ Time and Money: A well-planned design saves time and money in the long run."
"⚫ Growth: Understanding your role in the bigger picture helps you become a better designer."
""
"Exceptions:"
"⚫ The Factory: If you have a proven process and lots of experience, you might be able to jump straight into design."
"⚫ Experimentation: Sometimes, it's okay to just have fun and create for the sake of creating."
""
"Remember: While design is important, it's only one part of the puzzle. A strong strategy ensures that your work truly matters.

To write or not to write (withAI)?"
"Writing used to be a time-consuming craft—finding a compelling topic, researching, and creating engaging content. Hoping someone will read it and maybe even give it a thumb up. Good old days. 😅 "
""
"Now, with AI, you can generate posts in no time, often with impressive results, with almost no skill or effort at all. So why on earth should anyone write without AI? "
""
"The short answer: They shouldn’t. Even if a fast writer who cherishes creativity and the challenging process of writing, AI can elevate the work. Like spell check and autocorrect, AI will seamlessly integrate into the workflow, becoming your second brain (or hand)."
""
"The AI Scale"
"Understand the Spectrum: There’s ongoing debate about AI’s role in creativity. For writers, students, scientists, and creatives, the key is knowing how and when to use AI. The spectrum ranges from zero AI to full AI, with steps like ideation, research, feedback, writing, and rewriting in between."
""
"Delegate, Collaborate, Own"
"Strategic Use of AI:"
"👉 Delegate: Let AI handle tedious, time-consuming, or low-importance tasks."
"💪 Own: Keep control of tasks that are crucial, where you excel, or enjoy. Use AI for feedback."
"🤝 Collaborate: Find the right balance for tasks in between, finding the roght balance between effort, ownership, and efficiency."
""
"How I Write with AI"
"💡 Ideation: Ideas come easy, I have more then I can handle. AI helps generating variations and titles."
"📕 Research: AI provides quick summaries and diverse perspectives. Tools like Perplexity offer deep dives and fact-checking."
"✏ Writing: This is key activity for me that I don't delegate. It gives me control and helps organize my thoughts."
"📝 Editing: Before publishing, I ask AI for feedback to enhance clarity, length, and engagement without losing my voice. In this post I asked it to make it shorter and in format of a post on LinkedIn. "
""
"My own manifesto"
"Every post I post needs to include my tone of voice, ideas, and perspectives, enhanced by AI. I aim to be the primary creator, taking pride and responsibility for the final result. Without me, it’s just spam. "
""
"🌟 How are you integrating AI into your writing process? Share your thoughts below!

Process vs inspiration"
"Inspiration is great when it works but very unreliable as the only way to solving the problem. Instead of trying to find the perfect solution right away I started to see it like a journey, in other words a process. Knowing that I need a pool of info to build my ideas from, that the first drafts would be bad and I just needed to keep on trucking, not confusing my intuition with truth and getting a second opinions. Trusting that the process outcome will be solid, possibly even great. "
""
"What process you use (double diamond, design thinking or sprints) is less important than actually using a process. A process removes anxiety and pressure, instead focuses the energy on the task ahead, one step at a time. It helps with planing and time estimates. No more all-nighters just before the deadline. "
""
"The process should be a guiding light not a prison. The more you use it, the more flexible it gets. If you get an excellent idea right of the bat, go with flow. No need to push it through the whole system. But when the going gets tough, your inspiration has run dry and the deadline is getting closer, that’s when the process shines the brightest. "
""
"I used to believe that creativity and process were mutually exclusive. You could have one but not the other. Apparently I was wrong. When used in the right way, a process is a boost to productivity, creativity and getting a result that actually works. "
""
"#creativity #process #inspiration

The logical solution to solve heavy traffic is to build more and bigger roads. It makes perfekt sense. What we don’t include in the equation is that change to system leads to change in the behaviour. If there is more space, more people will drive and very soon we will end up where we started with even more people stuck in the heavy traffic. Our logical “solution” spend a lot of money and resources and make everything even worse. There is no shortage of similar projects. The road to hell is paved with good intentions."
""
"The same principle applies even to other areas. New solutions with new possibilities create new behaviours and set new expectations. What used to a luxury becomes a commodity, the magic turns into everyday tool. In other words, the wins and saves are only apparent in the time of transition. When everybody uses the new tech, it loses its competitive advantage. Those that don’t, won’t or can’t adapt will be left behind. "
""
"Until recently digitalisation was the promised land of efficiency and productivity. In certain area it delivered real value, in others the result was the opposite. Journalists were suddenly supposed to be photographers, photo editors, videographers and marketers. All that on top of their regular job with same resources and pay. Teachers whose main job is to teach should now also juggle a big administrative load. The vast amount of digital tools that were supposed to make this possible were (and still are) badly designed, built in silos, requiring double work. Other work areas were hit hard as well. The price of increased efficiency was payed by the workers, but the reward ended up somewhere else.  "
""
"AI is now following the same pattern. Every new AI startup boasts how much time and money their solution will save. In theory they are right. AI can be a huge productivity boost. For now. As soon as AI is on every app, tool and device and the productivity raises, the expectations and behaviours will follow. You will not get rest of the day off just because you let ChatGPT do the heavy lifting. You will get more tasks with expectations to do them even better, faster and cheaper. "
""
"So how can we make sure that those new (AI powered) solutions will actually reduce complexity and remove tedious task without adding on new ones? "
"The first part of the answer is not with more tech, but with better tech. Tech that doesn’t crave more attention and screen time. Instead it will allow users to spend more time away from screens with real humans creating real value through face to face connection and communication. "
""
"The second part is to stop chasing more productivity and efficiency. The power in AI should not be used to churn out more stuff nobody needs or asked for. "
""
"The last part is stop trying to solve organisational and systemic problems with new tech. New tech will not save education, welfare or sustainability. The tech is just a tool and enabler, not the solution.

""
"I’ve got the POWER"
"Helping puts you in a position of power. You are helpful and the other part is helpless. Be careful not to take advantage of this, creating dependance or felling that someone now owes you something. Helping should be a win-win, even on the emotional side."
""
"Learn people how to fish"
"Solving other peoples problems takes a lot of time and effort. There is a big risk that you will create learned helplessness among people you are trying to help. They will always come to you. First it is OK to tie your kids shoes. Then it’s better (for everyone) if you learn them how to do it. Same goes for your team. "
""
"What are you NOT doing when you are helping?"
"Helping others is great. Just be aware that it also means that you need to NOT do something that you intended to do. Unless your full-time job is helping people. Then keep doing that. "
""
"Conclusion"
"Helping others is great, we should all do more of that. Just make sure that your help is welcome, efficient and strengthen your emotional connection."
""
"#helping #coaching #leadership

Probably the best take on AI and Ethics I have seen. (Thought) provocative in both content and execution. 😅 "

I have spent lor of time searching for better answers. But what if I was wrong all along? What if I actually needed better questions?"
""
"I also overvalue my own answers and advice. Whenever someone is having a problem I’m there with my advice. Maybe I'm just trying to help. Maybe I (also) prove how smart I am. Giving advice puts me in a place of power and authority. Like a boss. 😎 "
""
"The more constructive (and humble) approach would be just to ask good questions and help the person to come up with the solution themselves. The probability for them following their own advice is much higher. So this approach is also much more impactful. "
""
"So take my advice and stop giving advice when nobody is asking you to. Even then, start with some good questions. Or what do you think?  "
""
"Read more in “The Coaching Habit” by Michael Bungay Stanier"
""

🌟 You want to make your portfolio shine, so you polish every pixel to perfection. Everything is nice and smooth, elegantly aligned in a beautiful harmony. But guess what. I’m not looking for perfection, because it sucks. Perfection takes a lot of time which often doesn’t exist. In the world where everything is changing at a breakneck speed, you don’t want to spend time to perfecting the wrong thing. "
""
"Perfection is static"
"Imagine that you actually achieve the perfect solution. Then comes your boss and says you need to change it. All that time and effort for nothing. A product is suposed to evolve and grow. How do you keep on iterating something that is already perfect? "
""
"Perfection is an illusion "
"Everyone has their own definition of perfection. A perfect product for the stakeholders looks different than a perfect product for the designers or the users. Whose perfections should you strive to fulfil? "
""
"Reality is messy"
"Back to your portfolio. I don’t want to see shiny postcards from an imagined future. I want to see how you tackle the messy reality with unexpected problems, impossible deadlines and demanding customers. I want to see you solving problems on the go, and adapting your pretty design to the harsh reality. "
""
"Attention to detail is important and being thorough is a superpower. Just don’t the strive for perfection get into you way. "
""
"#perfection #portfolio #design

Your portfolio is your portfolio"
"Well yeah… Duuuh… But wait. What I mean is that your portfolio is not just a container for your cases. The portfolio itself is a case study with design choices and execution. If you are just starting out, the portfolio might just be your first real project. So give it the attention it deserves. Use all the skills you are trying to show that you have. "
""
"You don’t need to make everything yourself or code it from scratch. Use Notion, Wix, Webflow, Squarespace or some other platform. Don’t make it harder than it is. Just make sure that you add something of you and your skills in the mix. Someone else’s template won’t tell me anything about you.  "
"#portfolio #jobtips #juniordesigner

🔚 Begin with the end in mind"
"This is one of the most basic yet most powerful frameworks. I see many teams skipping this step because it seems so obvious. Mostly that’s the reason why they fail. They don’t have a clear and united image of what they are trying to	accomplish and what kind of future tehy are creating."
""
"To do this exercise you don’t have to start with WHY, that is actually really hard. Just imagine that you arrived at your destination, your project is a success, you have cleared all the obstacles, finished on time and on budet. Everyone is happy, the stakeholders, the users, the team and even you. Describe how this future looks like. What is faster, harder, scooter, I mean better. What can users do now that they could not do before? What superpowers have they gained? How is this version of the future better the other versions? "
""
"🖼 Now create this image for your portfolio. What do you want the visitors to think and do when they enter your site and what should they take with them when they leave? What are the impressions you want to create? And how are they aligned with your work and personality? "
""
"👀 Close your eyes and be your own personal (design) Jesus. When you are back from the future, write down what you saw and the start figuring out how to get there.

😍 It’s a match"
"Another thing I wish I knew sooner: It is not the “most skilled” designer that is the best match. The hiring process is not a competition where the winner gets the job. When you don’t get hired it’s easy to start thinking that it’s your fault. That the others have something you lack. If you were a bit faster, more skilled, more experienced, more diverse or more specialised. If only…."
""
"No matter what level you or on, you will get a lot of rejections. It’s a part of the process. It’s not about winning over the other designers, it’s about finding and being a good match. Like finding a good pair of shoes that fits and feels just right. It’s nothing wrong with the other pairs, they are probably a perfect match for someone else, they just don’t fit your needs. "
""
"This doesn’t mean that you can just sit there and wait for your soulmate to find you. Just like with dating, you must make your own luck. Start with finding out who is a good match for you and how you can be a good match for them. Stop competing and start matching. "
""
"#hiring #jobapplicationtips #juniordesigner

🔮 A junior is hired for their future potential"
"This took me long time to realise and is something I wished I knew in my early days. Instead of feeling insufficient for lack of experience and real cases I would like to know that everyone started from zero. That someone helped me to embrace the state of not knowing anything about anything. "
"UX student is not a bad junior designer, and a junior designer is not an insufficient senior designer. Be the best in your class and be proud of that. See the higher levels as inspiration and aspiration, not competition. You are not bad, you are just not there yet. "
""
"Hiring a junior is a future investment that will take time and effort to shape. Make sure to show your potential but be also transparent on areas where you need guidance and support. Nobody expects you to hit the ground running. First you need to learn how to walk. "
"#juniordesigner #hiring #jobapplication

Portfolio series part3 : What does design mean to you?"
""
"There are many definitions of what design is, way of thinking, doing, making decisions, objects, aesthetics and so on.  So just asking “What is design?” is very broad and abstract. When reframing the question to “What does design mean to you?”, it becomes something more personal with no right and wrong answers. "
""
"❓ Why is this question relevant and how is it related to the portfolio?"
"When interviewing designers, I want to know what drives them and what they hope to create or accomplish. Is it to create nice and shiny objects, push the limits of UX and tech or perhaps fix things that are broken? I want to know if and how the designer reflects over their role and I want to see that being represented in the portfolio. This helps me to understand not just what a designer can do but why they are doing it. The thinking part is for me just as important as the doing part. "
""
"To find out what drives you take a short break and think about your dream project: "
"- What makes this project great? "
"- How are you making it great? "
"- Is it the journey, the output or the outcome that makes it awesome? "
"- How can you show this energy and motivation in your portfolio?"
""
"#design #portfolio #interactiondesign #jobinterview

Part 2 of the portfolio series:"
"The ideal portfolio when looking for an UX job is an intersection between what I as a designer have to offer and what the employer needs. The bigger the intersection, the greater are chances for landing the job. Or at least advancing to the next round. "
""
"💼 Does this mean that you need a unique portfolio for each job? "
"No, at least not completely. If you have a strong portfolio in a certain niche make sure to look for the jobs in that niche. Even a great portfolio can fail if it doesn’t feel relevant to the employer. Adapt the portfolios to the type of jobs you are looking for. If you are looking for very different types of jobs, like a freelance illustrator vs a UX employment, then it makes sense to have two different portfolios. The secondary skills are also important to give you a competitive edge or show your width. Just not at the expense of the skills that the employer is looking for."
""
"⚫  Drawing the cirlces"
"Start with your circle and think about your strengths as a designer and how that could be communicate in a portfolio. Where is it most likely that your skills will be valued? Where do you see yourself thriving and growing?"
""
"🔴 The other circle"
"Who is your audience? Is it a startup or a big corp? Their needs and expectations are probably very different. Find out what is important to them, how the hiring process looks like and what they want to see in a good portfolio. "
""
"⚖ The messy middle "
"Now it’s time to bring it all together. Figure out the best way to visualise your work, hopes and skills so that it resonates with your audience. Sketch, prototype, test and iterate. Connect with designers working at that or similar places and learn about their portfolios. Ask for advice and feedback on you portfolio."
""
"Building a portfolio is just like any other UX projects. You got this. 💪 "
"#portfolio #ux #jobsearch #hiring

Recently I held a short talk for UX students at Stockholm University. The purpose was to give the students a glimpse of the hiring process and what role the portfolios play when hiring designers. So I begun with a made up story:"
""
"Imagine you are about to go to a really important job interview but you get sick. Instead you send a friend. Yes it’s a bit unrealistic and maybe even illegal. But 🐻  with me. You don't just anyone. it has to be someone you trust. Who would you send, want should they say, how should they act and what should they show? You are not going to be there so you need to make sure that your friend does a good job representing you. The impression your friend will need to be aligned aligned with how you will act if you called to the next interview. "
""
"💌 Seeing your portfolio as an extension of you (not just a mail attachment) makes you care more. Care about what you say how and to whom. You will send it like a love letter not as worthless spam. "
""
"There was a lot more stuff and discussion, apparently I like talking. I will share that in next posts. Stay tuned. "
""
"🙏 Thanks a lot for the invitation Sharon Lindberg"
"and thanks a lot for great input for my research"

What is your portfolio's problem?"
"We take design tasks seriously. We have a design process, we use design thinking, empathy, we ideate, research, prototype, design sprint. We put a lot of work to make sure our product meets user needs. "
""
"Then we make a portfolio. 😬 "
""
"Suddenly we just “know” how it should look like and what jobs need to be done. We make a summary of our most recent work and add some reasoning that sounds smart. Plan B is just post some uncommented pretty pictures on Behance. We do everything that we tell our clients NOT to do. Because we are pros and this is just a portfolio."
""
"What if we take the UX approach? The portfolio is ABOUT us but it is not FOR us. There is purpose for its existence and needs to be met. Let's start with the usual questions:"
""
"- Why do you need a portfolio? "
"- How does the hiring process look like?"
"- What role does the portfolio play in that process?"
"- Who is going to use your portfolio and why?"
"- What is the best outcome (for you and the user)?"
"- How can you design towards that outcome?"
""
"When you have this mapped out you can start to design towards the desired outcomes. Intervju design and hiring managers in the firms and companies you are interested in. Find out what is important for them, what they expect from a portfolio and what would make the hiring process easier. Don’t pitch or sell. You are doing ux research not applying for a job. Not yet. "
""
"Finally. Your portfolio should be personal, highlight your skills and strengths in an interesting and novel way. Make your portfolio so good that it should be in your portfolio. 🙃 "
""
"But first and foremost it should help the hiring manager to quickly answer the crucial question: “Is this candidate relevant for the next step?""
""
"#portfolio #problemsolving #jobstobedone

🤷‍♂️ Business needs or Customer needs? "
"This choice used to be a no-brainer. My immediate response as UX designer was to obsessively focus on the user. Happy users are loyal customers and that is good for the business, right? With greater insight in the business part of the product, this choice is not that simple. What if the company needs to increase the revenue? How will a higher price benefit the customer?"
" "
"Even more (thought)provoking is reading Continuous Discovery Habits by Teresa Torres. She wants to start with the business needs first. "
""
"The reason for this is simple. Product teams not contributing to growing or improving the business will be short-lived. In other words, it doesn’t matter how much value you produce for the user if it doesn’t also show on the business side. "
""
"This makes total sense. It is like parents putting on the oxygen mask first, despite the urge to help the child. The designers just need to understand, accept and embrace this perspective and resist the “users first” instinct that has been so thoroughly engraved in our minds. This doesn’t mean that we need to abandon our mission to serve the customer and creating a great experience. But by building upon the needs of the business we also make sure to be around and create even more value, both for the business AND the customer. "
""
"🤘 In the long run, you don’t have to choose. "
""
"#productdesign #userexperience #needs #business

Control vs Convenience"
"No customer has ever asked for fewer functions. Companies and users believe that more features mean more opportunities, choices, and removing a feature can weaken the product. I call it Control first."
""
"It is the classical, tech-driven approach. Users have full control and responsibility. The quality of the outcome depends on their skills. If the outcome is bad, users just need to try harder. Complexity and responsibility lie with the user. The tool is just a tool."
""
""Control first" is perfect for advanced users who want precise adjustments and full control. They make all the decisions, have all the power and insight over the inputs, the process and the result. "
""
"Convenience, the easy way out"
""Convenience first" approach moves complexity and responsibility from users to the product. Users get the desired outcome with minimal effort, training, and skill. Automation, analytics, and AI predict user needs and preferences. Your phone knows you better than you know yourself."
""
"👑 Convenience is king for the majority of users. We can help new users to get up to speed with templates, assistants, and simplified GUI. We can automate or remove predictable and repetitive tasks, identify low-risk actions and make suggestions."
""
"Convenience requires trust. Users need to feel safe and know that the assistant (and company behind it) acts in their best interest. When all the decisions are made in a black box there is a risk of lack of transparency, control, and power."
""
"Summary"
"Convenience is the way to go but there needs to be a balance and collaboration between the user and the system. The system should ease the burden of everyday churns and also inform and educate the user about what decisions have been taken, why and what are the expected outcomes. The users should always feel that they are in charge and the system is working for them not instead of them.

The truth about the seat at the table"
"This mythical beast, the golden ticket, this promised land. So much has been said about the seat at the table, mostly by designers not even knowing what it means, or where and if the seat actually exist. I know, because I used to be one of them. It supposed to be the answer to all the problems. It would put the design on the map once and for all, giving designers a place they rightfully deserves. "
""
"It sounds almost religious, doesn’t it?"
""
"And guess what. I actually got it. I didn’t even have to fight for it, it was just there waiting. It was included in my new role at my new job. I just walked right in and took the seat. And it wasn’t exactly what I expected. "
""
"I thought that now I would have the power to put design first, give designers (and my self) more hours, make the design project more prioritised. More research, user testing, the whole design thinking package. "
""
"But now my perspective has shifted, I din’t just see all the trees. I saw the forests, hills and the horizon. Design was not the only or the most important part of the product. It was just one of several very important parts. It definitely needed attention and many improvements could be made to make it shine. But more design was not the solutions to every problem. "
""
"Suddenly it wasn’t just about me, as it used be when I was a contributor. I could use my power to improve my own situation, but that was not why I got the seat in the first place. It was to help the company and the product, not just designers and the design departement. "
""
"I will treat the seat and my new role as a tool. I’ll do my best to educate the company (and the customers) about the power of design. As soon you get a manager role, it’s not longer about you. It’s about the company, the product and the team. This is an insight that is not clearly communicated, but a key to do a good job when I finally have this seat.

"This mythical beast, the golden ticket, this promised land. So much has been said about the seat at the table, mostly by designers not even knowing what it means, or where and if the seat actually exist. I know, because I used to be one of them. It supposed to be the answer to all the problems. It would put the design on the map once and for all, giving designers a place they rightfully deserves. "
""

""

Predictable does not have to be boring"
"We designers want to be creative, innovative and push boundaries. Call us predictable and we will take it as an insult. No wonder that there was a pushback when design systems became popular. Why need designers if everything is already designed? More questions started popping up. "
" How can we merge creativity and predictability when they sound like complete opposites? Can we make new and exiting product with the same old design system or will everything just look the same?"
""
"Let’s find out. "
""
"Why is predictability important"
"First we need to acknowledge that predictability is a good thing. We want the trains to come on time and a file to be saved when we press the save-button. Predictability saves time, creates trust, enables teamwork and lets us fokus on less predictable problems. When we solve one problem we can apply the same solution to similar problems. The sense of quality is that it fulfils our expectations, is predictable. "
""
"Predictability in design"
"Spending a lot of time on creating and maintaining a design system instead of creating actual design feels contra productive. But a good design system saves time both for designers, developers and users in the long run. It creates a feeling that things belong together. It enables designers and developers to speak the same language and have the same source of truth, merging the gap between design sketches and design in code. "
""
"How to add creativity"
"Your product is not an advertising campaign that is short-lived and only relies on novelty and creativity. You play a long game and are creating a relationship with the users, making the experience as smooth as possible. That base functionality should not be tampered with."
""
"Still you want to add things that create delight, inspiration and deepen the emotional bond. Those things need to be added as a cherry on top, optional, easy to turn off or put aside. Like animated tutorials, micro animations, gamification or optional personalisation of the product."
""
"Don’t let the design system limit your creativity"
"Creativity can also materialised in other ways than by latest design trends. Have brainstorming workshops about new ways of solving user problems, build experimental prototypes or expand to new user groups. How would your product look like if it wasn’t on a screen, or just have voice control? "
"How is AI affecting what you can build and how you are building it? "
""
"🎨 Let creativity show you what is and can be possible. "
"🔄 Use predictability to build trust and user friendly products.

Bitching ain't switching"
"You know that friend that just complains but never does anything about it. Guess what. Users are the same. It is easy to believe that if you present a solution that is cheaper, easier, better, and smarter, all the unhappy users wills just jump over to your product. It makes sense. Unfortunately it’s far from the truth. "
""
"Why is that? "
"Glad you asked."
""
"Complaining is easy"
"Complaining doesn’t require a plan, action or risk taking. It is cope mechanism and a wish for things to be different. Maybe one day you or hopefully someone else will do something about it. But not today. "
""
"Old habits die hard"
"No matter how flowed the current product is, the users have learned a bunch of tricks and workarounds. They know what to expect, where to go fast and where to break. Better the devil you know, as they say."
""
"Sunken cost fallacy"
"All the money and time spent on training, tutorials and documentation. All the experts that know every power feature, and key shortcut. All the data collected and stored. What will happen to that? "
""
"Fear of the unknown"
"Who said that the new solution is going to be better? It looks nice and shine but will it really deliver on all the promises? And what new unknown problems will it bring? How long before we are up to speed? "
""
"The ecco system complexity"
"What if the product is a part of a system and connected to many other products? Haw do we make sure that the whole system just don’t break or creates bottlenecks in the production?How to unsure that all the connections will be recreated? "
""
"The lack of authority"
"Probably the most common reason for complaining and inaction. The users want to make a change but are not allowed. They are forced to use badly designed products. Someone else makes the decision, the pay the price. Complaining is the only authorised way the can protest and show what they think. "
""
"So what does this mean for designers?"
""
"Boost motivation, trust or ability"
"When stumbling on disappointed user in interviews, it is important to explore the whole picture. Just focusing on the problems in the current product is not enough. You need to understand the reason for the dissatisfaction and what makes it difficult to change. Is it a question of motivation, trust or ability? Your solution should support those needs to make the switch possible.

Start your new job by questioning your managers judgement. What were they thinking when they hired you? Were there really no better candidates? "
"Just kidding, kind of. Although getting to know the reason behind the hire is really important. "
""
"Why you, why now and what is the need you are supposed to fullfil? The hire is like a design problem. There is a need and a possible solution. If you suppose to be the solution to a problem it’s crucial to understand what the problem actually is. "
""
"It sounds like a no brainer but unclear expectations and job description is a really big problem. According to the State of design (2022) it is one of the biggest sources of stress among designers."
"https://lnkd.in/d3328gqv"
""
"So why it is so hard? Here are my best guesses: "
" "
"The management doesn’t know what it needs or what to expect"
"If you are hired by a non designer organisation, chances are that your manager is not a designer. The org knows that they need som kind of design but not how design work works. It.s really important that you, your team and the manager start to figure out what kind of design is needed and how it fits with current processes and roadmaps."
""
"They want to give you flexibility "
"This one is tricky. It either requires great knowhow and seniority from the new hire or a great deal of coaching. Probably both. Communication and collaboration is the key, so the right problem is solved in the right way. "
" "
"They hope that you, time or someone else will solve the problem"
"The team asked for a designer so the manager hired a designer. Problem solved. Or so it seems. With a bit of luck you and the team find a way to adapt and include design in the workflow. A less positive scenario is that the designer feels isolated from the team and lacks the power or confidence to change the situation."
""
"They believe that the job description in the add is enough"
"The job add is just a an add. It gives a short glimpse of a possible future and some expectations. But there are so many things left to discuss and explore. Things like culture are seldom documented but are crucial for a good fit. If it’s not part of the onboarding make sure that you adress it. "
""
"Ok, so back to the original question: "
""
"What were they thinking when they hired me ?"
"Of course I asked, both about the problem and why the thought that I was a good fit. Apparently I made a good impression during a hackathon and with my case study. My previous experience from ed-tech, product design and interest for AI was a great plus. But they also need someone unafraid of asking questions, challenging the status quo and taking the product design to the next level."
" "
" So my job description goes something like this. "
" “Do what you believe is right, improve what needs to be improved and help the company and the product to grow. We will let you know if you are out of bounds and help you back on track”."
" "
" Sounds good to me. 😅 "
"

Dealing with difficult clients"
"We have all been there. Impossible clients, making impossible demands, last minute changes, no feedback, bad briefs and stupid questions. Clients just being difficult, unpleasant, irritating and making your life a living hell. Just read all the “clients from hell” posts out there and you will you surely recognise several situations."
""
"What if"
"But what if are just falling for the good old Fundamental attribution error. What if it’s not the client that is difficult but their situation. What if they have a demanding boss, giving them impossible deadlines and last minute changes? If they are giving you a hard time, they probably have a hard time themselves. "
""
"Help me to help you"
"We seldom take time to see the whole picture. To help our clients we don’t just need to understand their request but also the need behind it. If you see that your client, customer or user is suffering, don’t blame the victim. Instead offer a helping hand. Show that you care not just about the project but also about them as people. "
""
"Pro tip"
"Try to use this skill on other “difficult” people like your manager, colleague or maybe your kids. Instead of blaming, start helping. I promise that something magical will happen. 🌈 "
""
"#clients #bias #help
